file_name: Sangeetha et al. - 2025 - Blockchain Integration for Multi-Skin Lesion Classification Advancing Diagnostic Security.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for multi-skin lesion classification (including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.) using dermoscopic images. It also employs federated learning for decentralized training and Explainable AI (XAI) for interpretability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original end-to-end deep learning system using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for multi-skin lesion classification. It describes specific methods including data preprocessing, federated learning, and blockchain integration, and reports original experimental results with performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC-ROC) in Table I. The abstract explicitly states 'In this paper, an end-to-end deep learning system... is presented,' indicating primary research rather than a literature review.

title: Blockchain Integration for Multi-Skin Lesion Classification Advancing Diagnostic Security
brief_summary: This study presents an end-to-end deep learning system using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for the classification of multi-skin lesions, including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, dermatofibroma, and actinic keratosis. The model was trained on a large dataset of publicly available dermoscopic images, which underwent preprocessing steps such as resizing, normalization, and augmentation to enhance generalization. To ensure data security and privacy, the system integrates federated learning for decentralized training and blockchain technology for data integrity and traceability. Explainable AI (XAI) techniques were employed to provide interpretability and transparency for clinical decision-making. The CNN model achieved high performance metrics, with precision, recall, and F1-scores ranging from 0.83 to 0.92 across the different lesion classes. The results indicate that the proposed AI-based platform offers a scalable, non-invasive, and secure solution for early skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the CNN model performance evaluation (Table I) with a clarity score of 92, precision of 0.91, recall of 0.92, and F1-score of 0.91. Depicted in Fig. 1.

all_task_types.target: Multi-skin lesion classes (Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis) and binary melanoma vs. non-melanoma status.
all_task_types.details: The paper presents a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for 'multi-skin lesion classification'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories including Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Actinic Keratosis. It also performs binary classification for 'melanoma and non-melanoma images'. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics: precision, recall, F1-score, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC-ROC (0.94). The loss function used was categorical cross-entropy, which is standard for multi-class classification.

datasets_used.name: Public Dermoscopic Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A large dataset of publicly available dermoscopic images obtained from trusted public databases. The dataset includes images of melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and benign nevi. It was collected to ensure wide coverage of different skin types, ethnicities, and ages, with labels provided by dermatologists.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model to predict melanoma and non-melanoma images, as well as multi-class classification of specific lesions.
use.validation: Used for validation during hyperparameter tuning and cross-validation (k=5) to prevent overfitting.
use.testing: Used for testing the predictive performance of the model using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
use.other: The dataset was preprocessed via resizing, normalization, and augmentation (rotation, brightness, contrast) to enhance generalization. Data augmentation techniques like oversampling, undersampling, and synthetic data generation were used to correct class imbalances.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To prevent overfitting and ensure model stability across different datasets.
To tune hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, number of convolutional layers, dropout rate) for model optimization.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC.

Public Dermoscopic Image Dataset.metric: Clarity, Precision, Recall, F1-Score (per class); Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, AUC-ROC (overall binary classification)
Public Dermoscopic Image Dataset.notes: The model was trained on a dataset of publicly available dermoscopic images including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and benign nevi. Performance metrics for Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Benign Nevi were not explicitly reported in Table I. The overall binary classification results (92% accuracy, etc.) are reported in the text under 'Experimental Results and Comparative Analysis'. The paper notes that the CNN-based model was superior to Support Vector Machines (SVMs) and Decision Trees in accuracy and computational expense.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning with pre-trained models was used to reduce training time and maximize feature extraction capability.

architecture_details.layers: Convolutional layers for feature extraction, pooling layers (max-pooling) for dimension reduction, and fully connected layers for classification.
architecture_details.training_method: Grid search for hyperparameter tuning and k-fold cross-validation (k=5) to prevent overfitting.

training_details.dataset: Public dermoscopic image dataset (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, benign nevi)
training_details.preprocessing: Resizing, normalization, augmentation (rotation, brightness, contrast), oversampling, undersampling, and synthetic data generation.

tasks: Multi-class classification (Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation, brightness alteration, and contrast adjustment were applied to dermoscopic images to enhance stability and accuracy.
Oversampling, undersampling, and synthetic data generation were used to correct class imbalances, specifically addressing the lower occurrence of melanomas compared to non-melanomas.

conclusions: The proposed end-to-end deep learning system using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) effectively enhances the early detection and classification of multi-skin lesions, including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, dermatofibroma, and actinic keratosis.

The CNN model achieved high diagnostic performance with an overall accuracy of 92%, precision of 91%, recall of 90%, F1-score of 90.5%, and an AUC-ROC of 0.94 for distinguishing melanoma from non-melanoma lesions.
Per-class performance metrics ranged from 0.83 to 0.92 for precision, recall, and F1-scores, with Dermatofibroma showing the highest clarity score (92) and Actinic Keratosis the lowest (84).
The integration of federated learning enables decentralized training that preserves patient confidentiality and data privacy without compromising model performance.
Blockchain implementation ensures data integrity, traceability, and transparency of patient records, addressing security concerns in dermatological diagnosis.
The use of Explainable AI (XAI) techniques improves the interpretability and reliability of model predictions, fostering trust in clinical decision-making.
The system reduces false positives and improves diagnostic efficiency compared to conventional approaches like visual inspection and invasive biopsies.
The proposed platform offers a scalable, non-invasive, and secure solution for skin cancer screening that is compatible with ethical and regulatory compliance.
Future improvements include the integration of smart contracts for blockchain integrity, multimodal data fusion (images and text), and adaptive learning loops to handle changing data distributions.
important_remarks: The dataset is described as 'publicly available' without specifying the exact source (e.g., ISIC, HAM10000), which limits reproducibility and assessment of population bias.

Performance metrics for Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Benign Nevi are mentioned in the dataset description but are missing from the reported results in Table I, creating a gap in the evidence for these classes.

The paper claims integration of federated learning and blockchain for security, but the experimental results section focuses solely on CNN classification accuracy without providing empirical evidence or metrics for the security, privacy, or blockchain components.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) is reported; performance claims rely on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) without significance testing.

The study uses transfer learning with pre-trained models but does not specify the base architecture (e.g., ResNet, VGG), which affects the assessment of computational efficiency and feature extraction capabilities.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; while k-fold cross-validation is used, the specific protocol for splitting public datasets (to avoid overlap between training and test sets from the same source) is not detailed.
The model's performance on 'non-melanoma' classes is aggregated in the binary classification results (92% accuracy), masking potential class-specific weaknesses within the non-melanoma group.

The paper mentions 'adversarial training' and 'multimodal data' (text/speech) in the methodology/future work sections, but these are not reflected in the reported experimental results, suggesting these features may not be fully implemented or evaluated in the current study.
